posts.name: Evan Shack
posts.cooked:
Celebrated Wine Critic Neal Martin Joins Vinous as Senior Editor
Departs from Robert Parker Wine Advocate to cover Bordeaux,
Burgundy, South Africa and New Zealand

New York, NY – 11/20/17 –Vinous, the world-renowned wine publication, is proud to announce that Neal Martin, the lead critic at Robert Parker Wine Advocate and one of the most recognized names in wine writing, is joining the Vinous team as a Senior Editor. “I have admired Neal’s passion, dedication, work ethic, talent and knowledge since I first met him in 2006, when we both worked alongside Robert Parker,” said Vinous CEO and founder, Antonio Galloni. “Neal brings to Vinous a unique combination of experience and wit that I am sure our readers will greatly enjoy. I am thrilled to be working alongside Neal once again.”

Starting February 2018, Neal Martin will add his incredible breadth of knowledge to Vinous’ roster of superstar wine critics that already includes Galloni, Stephen Tanzer, Josh Raynolds, Ian D’Agata and David Schildknecht. Vinous brings together the most trusted voices in wine writing and delivers their views to a global audience in a continual, daily stream of articles that breaks free from the constraints of traditional print media. In addition to reading Neal Martin’s reviews on the Vinous website, readers will be able to find his perspective on the Delectable wine app (which was acquired by Vinous in 2016), alongside over 5 million user ratings.

Neal Martin will cover the wines of Bordeaux, Burgundy, South Africa and New Zealand. With the addition of Martin to the team, Vinous will offer readers unprecedented insight into the wines of Bordeaux and Burgundy, both of which will be jointly covered by two leading authorities in wine. Martin and Galloni will each write separate, independent reports on the wines of Bordeaux, including en primeur, new releases from bottle, top value picks, and verticals and vintage retrospectives. Similarly, Neal Martin and Steve Tanzer will both cover Burgundy.

“Life should be about exploring possibilities. The prospect of joining Vinous raised so many that it was impossible to resist being a part of a truly world-class team,” said Martin. “I look forward to bringing my twenty years as a wine professional to Vinous and helping to shape its future as its next chapter opens. Palate and pen are primed and ready for action.”

posts.name: Stan Stevenson

posts.name: Cody Short
posts.cooked: Cancelled my subscription. Love what Vinous is doing and think the new approach is fantastic. Should be interesting!

I like Neal but the problem is that the model is pretty much dead. Nobody is waiting to price their wine until some lead critic opines. And it’s unlikely that such a critic will ever exist again - there are too many competing voices. Still, it’s interesting that Neal took a hike, given that at least WA seems to have some reasonably solid financing now that Michelin owns part of it. Must have really disliked the management? I wonder if they try to hire Burghound or some other PN guy.
Not an AG fan at all but this is a great move.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Wow.

Perhaps barring Robert Parker himself leaving to join Vinous, this has massive impact, considering that at various times both Galloni and Martin were seen as ‘heir apparent’.

I can’t see the balance of power slipping back to WA, though perhaps if they manage to take the lead in the Asian market, they may still be more financially successful?

I still have concern though, that sheep-like following of Parker created the monster that even had him calling the herd his ‘points whores’. Already we’re seeing Galloni anointing wines with 100 points and the wines triple (and more) in price overnight. The King is dead. Long live the King. I’d like to think the lessons of Parker’s dominance were learnt, but I suspect we’ll see Vinous become a similar behemoth,
I was wondering when this would happen. I am sure that Neal was chafing under Lisa esp with Bob out of the picture. I am not sure this will convince me to buy Vinous . . . but I’m thinking about it

In Oregon, where I had tasting appointments all last week I was reminded through conversations with vintners that since 2011, there have been four different people covering the state for TWA, half of them now write for Vinous.

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked:
Ken V:
GregT:

I like Neal but the problem is that the model is pretty much dead. Nobody is waiting to price their wine until some lead critic opines. …

Not sure how you can say this. Have you tried to buy 2013 Burlotto Monvigliero? 2013 Bartolo Mascarello or G. Rinaldi? I’ve been buying all these for years. Now with Antonio’s scores, they are $300 or more and still hard to find.

Ken, I think Greg’s point was priced from the winery, not what retailers and others are capitalizing on, as Bordeaux did when Parker was the king there. But you are 100% correct, Vinous scores ARE influencing the market. Maybe not in a wide spread panic kind of way that Parker did, but there is influence, and it’s growing. I had almost as hard of time getting my hands on 2013 Brovia Crus as I did Bartolo 2013. Something is really wrong there.

I think this was a brilliant move for both Neal Martin and Vinous. Join an A+ team, and jumping off of what is clearly a sinking ship. Two prominent critics leaving in one year is not a good look for WA.

Someone commented above about Tanzer retiring, and I believe that is 100% true, which is why Vinous could buy IWC.

posts.name: Mike Cohen

posts.name: Scott Everson
posts.cooked: The greatness of Bobby P continues, that he got out when he did.

“Martin and Galloni will each write separate, independent reports on the wines of Bordeaux, including en primeur, new releases from bottle, top value picks, and verticals and vintage retrospectives. Similarly, Neal Martin and Steve Tanzer will both cover Burgundy.”

Great approach…

+1

Mike Cohen:

I’m fascinated that vinous is making enough money to pay all of these guys? How profitable can it be?

I was just thinking the same thing…however, maybe they are not currently and are currently losing money as they scale/ramp up the team and business. Wasn’t there a thread about Vinous raising capital recently? Maybe AG is now just “buying” a first rate team with a plan to break even in the future. Really hard to imagine that it could possibly break even with such a large staff and duplicative tasting trips using a conventional wine review/subscription model so there will likely be other avenues of growth and revenue.

I really wonder what this means for the WA - with RP on the side lines currently and with the loss of Jeb and now Neal, WA now has a staff made up of little known reviewers and many regions covered by people without extensive experience reviewing those regions. Maybe WA is a strong enough brand to survive and stay relevant (maybe more so outside the US?) but in a environment there that seemed ripe for someone to grab the heir apparent label to lose Antonio, then Jeb and now Neal must mean there are significant management issues. If RP retires or cannot continue for health reasons they have no star, and more importantly no rising star, to take the healm and be the face of the WA. At this point, particularly without RP reviews, the relevance to me has slipped below the WS and probably even with WE, neither of which I pay any attention to.

Only reason to keep my subscription now is for older reviews…onto Jeb and Vinous for new reviews.

posts.name: Ken Vastola
posts.cooked:
Ian Dorin:

…Someone commented above about Tanzer retiring, and I believe that is 100% true, which is why Vinous could buy IWC.

I think this was the long term plan when Vinous bought IWC, but I doubt there is any set date. I had dinner with Steve earlier this year and the fire is still burning in him. I think the purchase by Vinous allowed him to off-load the administrative burden (and I would assume give him something financially for retirement), but I don’t think his retirement from wine writing is imminent.

Far bigger than the loss of any single reviewer is the trajectory. Bob was letting TWA go long before it was sold. The new owners could not only not stop the slide they couldn’t change the downward spiral. There’s a reason Galloni left. There’s a reason Dunnick left. There’s a reason Martin left. The underlying decay or cancer is now fully malignant and I don’t see it being stopped Michelin or no Michelin.

posts.name: Marc Hall
posts.cooked: TWA is sinking fast. I’ve been a subscriber for 10 years and agree with the sentiment that the business started going down hill when they made the bulletin board private. With the recent departures of Jeb and Neal coupled with the fact that Bob is no longer reviewing Napa, there is really no reason to remain a subscriber. Unfortunately, my palate doesn’t seem to align that well with Antonio on California wines, Suckling tends rate things 3 or 4 points too high(esp value wines) and Jeb is an unknown on Napa although I do like his Rhone and Central Coast reviews. I already subscribe to Jeb’s site and will probably subscribe to Vinous for Bdx coverage. I do like Vinous’ video content.

posts.name: Ben Giliberti
posts.cooked:
S. Stevenson:

WA>Vinous in terms of world wide influence still. I doubt that changes anytime soon.

That’s the big question to me. The high end Bordeaux market really depends on the answer to this. WA’s role was like that of certified coin-grading in the numismatic market. They both created booming investment markets in these “collectibles” by standardizing the valuation metric. I was surprised at how successfully the WA sustained its influence when Parker retired from BDX. No doubt this was due to the credibility that NM gained over the years, as he wrote parallel reviews with Bob. But who, if anyone, is ready, willing, and able to assume the mantle now?

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Mason H:

I have no inside knowledge, but this would be far from the first time a company is purchased, driven into the ground by new owners, and sold at a significant discount.

Or just closed completely. Happens all the time.

Alan Rath:
Mason H:

I have no inside knowledge, but this would be far from the first time a company is purchased, driven into the ground by new owners, and sold at a significant discount.

Or just closed completely. Happens all the time.

It would be a savvy move for AG to buy TWA at a sharp discount, and it wouldn’t surprise me if the investment group behind TWA were looking to cut losses on what has been a miserable investment. The inventory of past notes would be a huge resource.

And it is hard to believe that Lisa is still employed. What value, exactly, has she brought to the enterprise? Aside from the sake adventure and the pay-for-our-dimwitted-winemaking-advice debacle. And the insulting attitude that passes for “customer service.” She owns all of that

Ian Dorin:
Ken V:
GregT:

I like Neal but the problem is that the model is pretty much dead. Nobody is waiting to price their wine until some lead critic opines. …

Not sure how you can say this. Have you tried to buy 2013 Burlotto Monvigliero? 2013 Bartolo Mascarello or G. Rinaldi? I’ve been buying all these for years. Now with Antonio’s scores, they are $300 or more and still hard to find.

Ken, I think Greg’s point was priced from the winery, not what retailers and others are capitalizing on, as Bordeaux did when Parker was the king there. But you are 100% correct, Vinous scores ARE influencing the market. Maybe not in a wide spread panic kind of way that Parker did, but there is influence, and it’s growing. I had almost as hard of time getting my hands on 2013 Brovia Crus as I did Bartolo 2013. Something is really wrong there.

I think this was a brilliant move for both Neal Martin and Vinous. Join an A+ team, and jumping off of what is clearly a sinking ship. Two prominent critics leaving in one year is not a good look for WA.

Someone commented above about Tanzer retiring, and I believe that is 100% true, which is why Vinous could buy IWC.

There is influence, but it’s not based on a single critic. The Mascarello and Monvigliero are expensive in all vintages. Even when Parker anointed a new winery and gave a vintage 100 points, the older vintages wouldn’t necessarily move. And Bordeaux still is the wine that matters in the market - it’s the most widely collected benchmark wine. Peers and bloggers and free lancers have more influence than they ever did, every day another celebrity decides to get into the wine business, and there are new critics popping up all over. Add to that the fact that there’s more wine from more places and in more styles than ever and the market is becoming more like the market for music or other entertainment. There won’t be another Johnny Carson or Walter Cronkite. People will follow critics they happen to like.

I would argue that of everyone today, Suckling probably has more market influence, and of course Asimov.

As far as Vinous buying any other publication - why? W/out Parker, the WA is meaningless. You notice that they always call it the Robert Parker Wine Advocate, it’s never just the WA any more. Without his name, it doesn’t matter, no matter how good the writers may be. And FWIW, Mark Squires is a pretty good critic.

Bob had a good run and in the unlikely event that he returns on any kind of a regular basis, even his influence will be lessened, although I suppose he could find some obscure CA or Bordeaux producer and make them wealthy.

None of that means the critics aren’t worth reading if that’s your thing. But Parker could make a wine sell out practically overnight. That’s just not happening any more.

GregT:

As far as Vinous buying any other publication - why? W/out Parker, the WA is meaningless. You notice that they always call it the Robert Parker Wine Advocate, it’s never just the WA any more.

Because you’d be buying decades of Parker notes that would show up on your site now, as well as acquiring many years of notes by one of your current writers’ work product

posts.name: Ian Dorin
posts.cooked: Something crossed my mind, and it’s kind of interesting as there have been a number of posts about “how important Parker is”. I’ve spoken to a number of winemakers over the years, and it’s kind of shocking how few people sign up for a list when Parker rates something well (meaning, not 100 points). Numbers are typically between 7 and 10 at best, which is startling. What’s worse is that those that who sign up for lists that aren’t full and received 100 points, is that those people are loyal to the numbers, and aren’t interested long term in those wines. Frankly, I’d rather be making good wine with a loyal following (a la Myriad, MACDONALD, et al) than making 100 point wine where people are going to hit it and quit it.

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked:
Wilfred,

how are you doing?

I don’t know if the 130.000 new subscribers is a myth. I have no clue. But with all the efforts of WA in China, Singapore etc. without any success something must have gone terribly wrong. Parker was there many times to strengthen the brand. And China etc. was/is very interested in western lifestyle as we all experienced so wine etc. is an issue over there. If the Chinese investors are no fools – and I guess they weren’t – they must have seen some potential for WA in their region. And at least some success is likely IMO. It is also possible that Neal had some personal issues with Lisa and he left the boat therefore. But I admit this is pure speculation.

“Personal issues” I guess for both Neal and Antonio and the others who left.

I’m sure they picked up subscribers in Asia. But it’s one thing to say they picked up subscribers. It’s quite another to say it brought in $13M.

You believe they aren’t fools but that’s also an assumption. I’m not sure if you followed on the Cellartracker forum or not somebody was hawking s book (was it by Lisa PB?). They asserted they were just newly into wine and loved the book and urged people to buy it. Eric LeVine traced the IP address to the TWA in Asia. Talk about a dumb move! Kind of behavior of a fool.

https://www.cellartracker.com/forum/printable.asp?m=372690

Great story!

The China angle is the only one that makes sense. But as they grow increasingly confident, why wouldn’t they develop their own brand? Maybe there is an opportunity for a Chinese version of Parker, talking to people as they’re learning about wine. The US is past that.

Something crossed my mind, and it’s kind of interesting as there have been a number of posts about “how important Parker is”. I’ve spoken to a number of winemakers over the years, and it’s kind of shocking how few people sign up for a list when Parker rates something well (meaning, not 100 points). Numbers are typically between 7 and 10 at best, which is startling. What’s worse is that those that who sign up for lists that aren’t full and received 100 points, is that those people are loyal to the numbers, and aren’t interested long term in those wines. Frankly, I’d rather be making good wine with a loyal following (a la Myriad, MACDONALD, et al) than making 100 point wine where people are going to hit it and quit it.

Ian - good point but it’s only one segment of the market. Harlan, Colgin, Bryant, etc., are Parker creations. SQN too. There aren’t mailing lists in Europe that are the same as here - in many countries you can buy directly fairly easily. But Parker’s influence on the Rhone and Bordeaux is unique. In Bordeaux, they relied on their classifications. Initially he allegedly ignored those; ultimately he seemed more interested in validating them. His influence on the Rhone, particularly the south, was far more profound. There is no critic who can bring that kind of attention to a region these days - on the other hand there are trendies who seek out regions that are new to them and proclaim those regions as brilliant discoveries.

I can see China being a market to some degree, but then why did the WA need the Michelin money if cash flow was positive? As to the value of old notes - I don’t see it. Who reads old tasting notes that are irrelevant 10 or 20 years later? The scores matter far more and those are easily picked up from many public sites.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Jürgen Steinke:

I simply assumed that someone who made millions and spending big money in buying WA couldn’t be a fool. But who knows – maybe I am wrong here. Maybe I am actually naive but I always thought if you not win the lottery it isn’t that easy to make millions and you have to be more clever than 95% of the population. But wait a minute – some inherit millions. In this case your IQ can be below 100 …

My understanding is that investors bought WA and Lisa not being one of them? She is the hired CEO? Is that the case? If Lisa wrote the foolish post … well … you are right … that was foolish indeed and marketing for beginners …

Buying businesses is a risky business, even if you’re smart. And there’s a long, long history of buyers on one continent making very foolish purchases on another continent, particularly when they think they have a vision for the business that the old owners didn’t have.

The risks are only compounded when the real assets are people. The WA’s subscription list is worth something, but nowhere near what they paid for the business.

posts.name: Ken Vastola
posts.cooked:
P.Sunesen:

Latest news:

Another Brit, William Kelly will be his successor for Burgundy et al, albeit not for Bordeaux, which will be covered by Lisa Perotti Brown.

I’m personally particularly excited by the former choice, as I hold William K. in high esteem both as a reviewer and as a writer

Peter

Also, Central Coast and Washington:
“William Kelley, who will join the reviewer team on January 1, covering the regions of Burgundy, Chablis, Beaujolais, California Central Coast and Washington State.”

Sounds like some people have heard of William Kelly, but I don’t think I have come across him or his reviews at all - don’t think I’ve heard the name even. I wonder what percentage of the WA’s subscriber base will be willing to stick around for a trial and error period of what could be years with a team without much (public/well known) history at all in the regions they are reviewing. No matter how talented the team may be, how many people are going to buy based on the reviews of reviewers that they don’t know?

Maybe enough people are just interested in the reviews in and of themselves and don’t think more about it and so they won’t feel a sting from lost subscribers…just hard to put a read on it. If RP is able to get out there and provide reviews for a while longer, maybe fewer people will drop but if he isn’t, I am just not sure what the value of the subscription is other than the database of prior reviews. I suspect that both Vinous and Jeb and probably Burghound as well will have a decent influx of subscribers in the next couple months.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Thanks to everyone for the kind words!

Certainly hoping to continue to post here.

Anyone curious about my work will be able to have a look at some 60,000 words of commentary and tasting notes on the 2016 Burgundy vintage, which will be online with Decanter before the end of the year.

William Kelley:

Thanks to everyone for the kind words!

Certainly hoping to continue to post here.

Anyone curious about my work will be able to have a look at some 60,000 words of commentary and tasting notes on the 2016 Burgundy vintage, which will be online with Decanter before the end of the year.

Congratulations

In spite of so many imperfections, I have decided to remain loyal to TWA.

A short while ago I had to subscribe to Jeb Dunnucks site when he left…

And, by Jove, now also to Vinous because I’m kind of addicted to Neal Martins writing…and then you tell me to splurge on Decanter (which as a matter of fact was the first wine publication I started reading, when I was first bitten by the grape bug in the late seventies)?

It must certainly be a momentary lapse of reason from your new employers not to specify their politics regarding this forum?
However, one could secretely hope that their stubborn attitude to this pet aversion is on the retreat.

In any case:
see you soon on the Bulletin Board

Peter

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked: Grammatically, the title with the possessive brands the journal as belonging to Parker, although this possession need not be financial (it could mean that the journal follows his original conception). The second title turns Parker’s name into a neutral title that might mean anything or nothing.If one cares to perhaps overread the significance of the title change, it could be taken to signify a further breaking of the journal from Parker’s aegis or a recognition that that break has already taken place. Or not.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Jonathan Loesberg:

Grammatically, the title with the possessive brands the journal as belonging to Parker, although this possession need not be financial (it could mean that the journal follows his original conception). The second title turns Parker’s name into a neutral title that might mean anything or nothing.If one cares to perhaps overread the significance of the title change, it could be taken to signify a further breaking of the journal from Parker’s aegis or a recognition that that break ha already taken place. Or not.

To my mind, if they were going to change the name, changing it to The Wine Advocate was the sensible thing to do. He’s not running it any more, the poor fellow doesn’t appear to have the health to play a significant role (even if he wanted to and I’m not at all convinced that is the case), and I fully expect him to retire as soon as the relevant clause in the sale of the business expires. Going the whole hog would have sent a more positive message that they trusted in the future, rather than relying on the name of the founder and the fading influence of that name.

fancy_title: Neal Martin leaves WA FOR VINOUS
title: Neal Martin leaves WA FOR VINOUS
